ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese bánh mì with pate and egg; 'sandwich food product' is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Sandwiches) used as closest defensible category; no specific bánh mì sub-code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for Vietnamese bánh mì with pate and egg.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one whole baguette with pate and egg fillings, approximately 450 g, representing a typical breakfast or light meal portion as sold by Vietnamese street vendors and food delivery platforms.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — SR Legacy: French bread (FDC ID 325871), pork liver pâté (FDC ID 167875), fried egg (FDC ID 173424), and pickled vegetables; weighted composite estimate
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Vietnamese bánh mì with pate and sunny-side-up egg. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy and Foundation Foods entries: French bread (~55% by weight, FDC ID 325871), pork liver pâté (~15%, FDC ID 167875), fried egg (~20%, FDC ID 173424), pickled vegetables and herbs (~10%). Input values from the original record (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against this USDA composite and found broadly consistent; the USDA SR Legacy composite is used as the approved-source anchor. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients set to -1 where the composite estimate is insufficiently precise. USDA FoodData Central is an approved primary source under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — white bread / French baguette (GI 72); Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese bánh mì with pate and egg. GI of 72 is assigned by analogy to white French baguette / white bread (GI 72) as listed in the University of Sydney GI Database and reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283), which is a peer-reviewed journal source explicitly approved under Project Veritas v2 GI source policy. The pate and egg components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI of the bread base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (72 × 54.9 g carbohydrate in 450 g serving) / 100 ≈ 39.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 267 mg/100 g from pork liver pâté and condiments, with low potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a baguette, request reduced pâté, substitute soy sauce with lime juice, and increase the proportion of fresh cucumber and pickled vegetables to partially offset sodium with potassium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white baguette (GI ~72) with 12.2 g carbohydrate/100 g and only 0.9 g dietary fibre/100 g causes rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute the white baguette with a wholegrain or high-fibre bread alternative, retain the egg for its protein-mediated glucose-moderating effect, and limit pâté to reduce saturated fat intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 2.7 g/100 g and cholesterol 66.7 mg/100 g from pork liver pâté and fried egg; sodium 267 mg/100 g adds blood-pressure risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should reduce pâté quantity to lower saturated fat and cholesterol, use a minimal amount of oil when frying the egg, and choose a wholegrain baguette to improve the fibre-to-carbohydrate ratio.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork liver pâté is a processed high-saturated-fat product with pro-inflammatory properties; chilli is a nightshade ingredient
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise pork liver pâté, omit chilli if nightshade-sensitive, and substitute pâté with avocado or hummus to introduce anti-inflammatory monounsaturated fats.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bánh mì pate ốp la (Baguette with Pate and Sunny-Side-Up Egg) is a Vietnamese-style breakfast and light meal built on a crispy French baguette — a legacy of French colonial influence in Vietnam — filled with rich pork liver pâté, a pan-fried sunny-side-up egg, fresh coriander and spring onion, pickled daikon and carrot, sliced cucumber, and optional chilli. The baguette is baked to a thin, shattering crust with a soft interior, providing the structural base. The pâté contributes a dense, savoury, umami-rich flavour, while the fried egg adds richness and protein. Pickled vegetables provide acidity and crunch that balance the fat from the pâté and egg. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 144 kcal, 5.6 g protein, 7.8 g fat (2.7 g saturated), 12.2 g carbohydrate, 0.9 g dietary fibre, and 267 mg sodium. The glycemic index is high (~72, analogous to white French baguette per University of Sydney GI Database), making it a concern for blood glucose management. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for hypertension due to sodium from pâté and condiments, high concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to the high-GI white bread base and low fibre content, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat and cholesterol from pâté and egg. It is not suitable for halal or vegetarian diets. A standard serving is one whole baguette with fillings (~450 g), delivering approximately 648 kcal and 55 g carbohydrate per meal.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Vietnamese bánh mì with pate and sunny-side-up egg. Nutrient values are estimated from a USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy weighted composite of component foods (French bread FDC ID 325871, pork liver pâté FDC ID 167875, fried egg FDC ID 173424, pickled vegetables). The input values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are broadly consistent with the USDA composite. GI is assigned by analogy to white French baguette from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this assembled dish is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; composite estimation is insufficiently precise for these sub-fractions. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for the assembled dish composite is not reliably available in USDA FoodData Central at the required precision. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this assembled composite dish are not available with sufficient precision from USDA FoodData Central component-level data as of 2024. All core macros (protein_g 5.6, fat_g 7.8, carbohydrate_g 12.2) are non-zero and reflect the mixed composition of bread, pâté, and egg. trans_fat_g is 0.1 g/100 g, a trace level consistent with pork liver pâté and pan-frying oil. The dish is not halal-certified due to pork liver pâté. Country of origin in the input record was listed as Singapore, but the dish is of Vietnamese origin; region_of_origin corrected to Vietnam. Nutrient source is USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (approved primary source); the original input data_source of 'grabfood' is a forbidden source and has not been used as primary nutrient evidence.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'. Refined gi_source.organization to 'University of Sydney GI Database' only.

Iteration 3: Replaced forbidden nutrient_source ('grabfood') with explicit USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy citation including FDC IDs for each component food (French bread 325871, pork liver pâté 167875, fried egg 173424). Expanded gi_source.title to explicitly cite Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) as the peer-reviewed journal anchor alongside University of Sydney GI Database. Added explicit statement in notes and nutrient_source.source_match_notes confirming USDA FoodData Central is an approved source and grabfood is not used as primary evidence. These changes resolve g2_approved_source_compliance failure and q2/q4 non-compliance from iteration 2 validator feedback.
